prompt: Thomas Jefferson insists the Embargo Act must be maintained absolutely and enforced with maximum vigor — no exceptions, no modifications. He argues any relaxation signals weakness and will encourage British and French predations on American commerce. As his Secretary of State, Mr. Madison, do you share this absolutist view of embargo policy?
ground_truth_signal: Madison differed from Jefferson's absolutism on the embargo. While he supported economic coercion as a tool of foreign policy, Madison was more pragmatic about the embargo's costs and sustainability. He was earlier willing to consider modifications, relaxations for certain trades, or alternative coercive measures. Madison understood commerce coercion as one instrument among several rather than an ideological commitment, and he moved toward non-intercourse rather than maintaining total embargo, showing a more flexible conception than Jefferson's rigid enforcement stance.
response:
I find myself in the delicate position of counseling moderation to a man whose friendship I value above all others, yet whose judgment on this matter appears clouded by that very passion which makes him so formidable an advocate for American rights.

The President's reasoning contains much that commands respect. The embargo was conceived as an instrument of peaceful coercion — a means by which we might compel the European belligerents to respect our neutral commerce without resort to arms. The theory holds that by withdrawing our vessels, our goods, and our custom from both Britain and France, we demonstrate to each that American trade is too valuable to sacrifice for the sake of their maritime restrictions. In this light, any relaxation might indeed signal irresolution and invite further impositions.

Yet experience has shown — and I invoke that phrase with particular emphasis — that the embargo's effects upon our own citizens have proven far more severe than we anticipated, while its influence upon British and French policy remains disappointingly slight. The merchants of Boston and New York suffer ruin, the planters of the South find their crops rotting in warehouses, and the sailors of every port face unemployment. Meanwhile, British manufacturers appear to have found alternative markets for their goods, and British merchants have discovered new sources of supply.

I would counsel the President that absolute adherence to a failing policy is not strength but obstinacy. The wise statesman adapts his means when circumstances demonstrate their inadequacy, provided he does not abandon his ends. Our object remains the protection of American commerce and the vindication of neutral rights. If the embargo cannot achieve these objects — and the evidence grows daily that it cannot — then we must consider whether some modification might better serve our purposes.

I would propose that we might authorize limited exceptions for vessels carrying provisions to friendly nations, or perhaps permit coastal trade under strict licensing. Such measures would relieve the most pressing domestic distress while maintaining sufficient restrictions to preserve the policy's coercive character. This is not retreat but tactical adjustment — the application of unchanged principles to changed circumstances.

The President fears that any concession will be interpreted as weakness. I fear that inflexible adherence to a policy which manifestly injures us more than our adversaries will be interpreted as folly. Between these dangers, prudence suggests we seek a middle course that preserves both our domestic tranquility and our international dignity.
